description: To undergo an adverse change that leaves condition or function worse than before. A cracked handle or a flooded garden can each 'pukeri' while remaining recognizably itself. A damaged thing may remain whole; 'pukate' (break) names the more complete loss of wholeness.
articulatory_notes: Close the lips for 'p' and release into rounded high-back 'u'. Press the back of the tongue against the soft palate for 'k', then release into stressed true-mid front 'e'. Finish with a tap or short trill at the alveolar ridge followed by high-front 'i'.
sound_symbolism: The shared opening 'pu' gives 'pukeri' an audible kinship with 'pukate' (break). 'pukate' continues through two more stops; 'pukeri' leaves the velar release for a brief tap, which makes the word feel less final.
usage_notes: The affected thing is the subject at base. Causative 'ka' adds the agent or force responsible, and the event-noun rule supplies damage as a noun.

pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: The intransitive base can report the changed condition before a speaker knows its cause; responsibility remains available through the causative or a separate clause.

semantic_domains.physical: adverse change to the condition or function of a tangible thing
